---
title: "Unit-Level Estimation"
---

Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly rather than starting from domain-level direct estimates. They can be powerful, but they require much stronger data conditions than area-level Fay-Herriot models. 

## Understanding Unit-Level SAE

In area-level SAE, the model starts from one direct estimate per domain. In unit-level SAE, the model starts from individual or household survey records:

$$
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
$$

where $y_{di}$ is the outcome for unit $i$ in domain $d$, $x_{di}$ is a vector of unit-level covariates, $u_d$ is the area effect, and $e_{di}$ is unit-level noise. For binary outcomes, the model is usually written as a logistic or probit mixed model.

The domain estimate is then built by predicting or simulating values for the population units in each domain and aggregating them:

$$
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
$$

This makes unit-level SAE a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, then aggregate to the geographic domain of choice.

## Pros and Cons

Unit-level SAE is useful when the survey and population data share comparable individual, household, or poststratification covariates.

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Can aggregate estimates to multiple geographies | Needs detailed population data, not just area averages |
| Can use survey microdata directly without aggregating to domains | Survey and population variables must be coded in the same way |

The main barriers to unit-level estimation are microdata availability and privacy. In general, use area-level SAE when only domain-level covariates are available (more common). Unit-level SAE becomes more realistic when suitable population microdata, or a synthetic population, can provide the prediction population.

## Code Implementation

Use [`SUMMER::smoothUnit()`](https://search.r-project.org/CRAN/refmans/SUMMER/html/smoothUnit.html) to fit a basic unit-level SAE model from a `survey::svydesign()` object and population unit-level covariates. It supports Gaussian or binomial outcomes and can optionally use an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.

```{r}
library(survey)
library(SUMMER)

# survey_microdata should contain:
# - indicator
# - domain_id
# - weight and survey design variables
# - unit-level covariates used in the model
#
# population_microdata should contain:
# - domain_id
# - the same unit-level covariates for the target population

design <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~psu,
  strata = ~stratum,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_microdata,
  nest = TRUE
)

unit_fit <- SUMMER::smoothUnit(
  formula = outcome ~ age_group + employment_status + car_access,
  domain = ~domain_id,
  design = design,
  family = "binomial",
  X.pop = population_microdata,
  adj.mat = NULL,
  level = 0.95
)
```

### Worked example

N/A
